name: Platform Power Is Underrated
abstract: This article delves into the long-standing debate surrounding Apple's App Store policies, arguing that while initially a 'geeky obsession,' the App Store has become a critical tech story highlighting anti-competitive practices. It references Lord Acton's quote on power's corrupting influence and details the author's journey from misjudging the unsustainability of Apple's model to acknowledging its immense profitability and sustainability due to user demand. The piece analyzes Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers' ruling against Apple's anti-steering provisions, discussing its implications for developers and users, and how it will impact Apple's revenue. It redefines the 'chicken-and-egg problem' of platform building, asserting that user acquisition is paramount, driving developer engagement regardless of barriers. Ultimately, the author proposes a legislative solution to ensure economic and political freedom for third-party applications on large platforms, arguing that such regulation is in the public interest despite potentially diminishing platform providers' earnings, as these platforms derive their power from user demand.
